大和神社 | image = File:Oyamato-jinja, haiden.jpg | caption = The ''Haiden'', or main prayer hall. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57083333|N|135.8375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|qid=Q3028663}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|qid=Q11387080}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 306 Hoshiyama, Shinsencho, [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] | website = {{URL|http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|qid=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Tenri|qid=Q502196}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Shrine'''|大和神社|''Ōyamato Jinja''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Monument of Japanese battleship Yamato in Oyamato Shrine.jpg|thumb|262px|Monument of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]] in Ōyamato Shrine]] In the time of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] there was a crisis. Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]], the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace. When a series of plagues broke out during [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin's]] reign, he "dreaded [...] the power of these Gods, and did not feel secure in their dwelling together." He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter Toyosukiirihime, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and delegated the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] to another daughter, {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}},{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became [[:en:Emaciated|Emaciated]] after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |issn=0304-1042}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]].{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed [[:en:Futomani|divination]], which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased. After this [[Ōyamato Shrine]] was founded for Yamato Okunitama{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] }} [[en:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[ja:大和神社]] [[ja:Ōyamato shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|大和神社|version=100985927|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] }} [[en:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[ja:大和神社]] [[ja:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[zh:大和神社]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata [[sn:commons:Category:Ohyamato-jinja]][[en:Ōyamato Shrine]][[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]][[ja:大和神社]][[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]][[zh:大和神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q245731]] Wikidata name: Ōyamato Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōyamato Shrine
大和神社 | image = File:Oyamato-jinja, haiden.jpg | caption = The ''Haiden'', or main prayer hall. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57083333|N|135.8375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|WD=Q11387080}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 306 Hoshiyama, Shinsencho, [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] | website = {{URL|http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Shrine'''|大和神社|''Ōyamato Jinja''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Monument of Japanese battleship Yamato in Oyamato Shrine.jpg|thumb|262px|Monument of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]] in Ōyamato Shrine]] In the time of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] there was a crisis. Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]], the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace. When a series of plagues broke out during [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin's]] reign, he "dreaded [...] the power of these Gods, and did not feel secure in their dwelling together." He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter Toyosukiirihime, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and delegated the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] to another daughter, {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}},{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became [[:en:Emaciated|Emaciated]] after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |issn=0304-1042}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]].{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed [[:en:Futomani|divination]], which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased. After this [[Ōyamato Shrine]] was founded for Yamato Okunitama{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|大和神社|version=100985927|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] }} [[en:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[ja:大和神社]] [[ja:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[zh:大和神社]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter Toyosukiirihime, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and delegated the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] to another daughter, {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}},{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became [[:en:Emaciated|Emaciated]] after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |issn=0304-1042}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]].{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed [[:en:Futomani|divination]], which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased. After this [[Ōyamato Shrine]] was founded for Yamato Okunitama{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|大和神社|version=100985927|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] }} [[en:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[ja:大和神社]] [[ja:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Tenri, Nara]] [[Category:History of Tenri, Nara]] [[Category:Yamanobe-no-michi]] {{Commons category|Ohyamato-jinja}} [[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]] [[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[zh:大和神社]] {{Commons category|Ohyamato-jinja}} [[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]] [[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[zh:大和神社]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q245731}} [[sn:commons:Category:Ohyamato-jinja]][[en:Ōyamato Shrine]][[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]][[ja:大和神社]][[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]][[zh:大和神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q245731]] Wikidata name: Ōyamato Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōyamato Shrine
大和神社 | image = File:Oyamato-jinja, haiden.jpg | caption = The ''Haiden'', or main prayer hall. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57083333|N|135.8375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|WD=Q11387080}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 306 Hoshiyama, Shinsencho, [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] | website = {{URL|http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Shrine'''|大和神社|''Ōyamato Jinja''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Monument of Japanese battleship Yamato in Oyamato Shrine.jpg|thumb|262px|Monument of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]] in Ōyamato Shrine]] In the time of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] there was a crisis. Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]], the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace. When a series of plagues broke out during [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin's]] reign, he "dreaded [...] the power of these Gods, and did not feel secure in their dwelling together." He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter Toyosukiirihime, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and delegated the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] to another daughter, {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}},{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became [[:en:Emaciated|Emaciated]] after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |issn=0304-1042}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]].{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed [[:en:Futomani|divination]], which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased. After this [[Ōyamato Shrine]] was founded for Yamato Okunitama{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and [[sn:commons:Category:Ohyamato-jinja]][[en:Ōyamato Shrine]][[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]][[ja:大和神社]][[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]][[zh:大和神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q245731]] Wikidata name: Ōyamato Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōyamato Shrine
大和神社 | image = File:Oyamato-jinja, haiden.jpg | caption = The ''Haiden'', or main prayer hall. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57083333|N|135.8375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|WD=Q11387080}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 306 Hoshiyama, Shinsencho, [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] | website = {{URL|http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Shrine'''|大和神社|''Ōyamato Jinja''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Monument of Japanese battleship Yamato in Oyamato Shrine.jpg|thumb|262px|Monument of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]] in Ōyamato Shrine]] In the time of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] there was a crisis. Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]], the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace. When a series of plagues broke out during [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin's]] reign, he "dreaded [...] the power of these Gods, and did not feel secure in their dwelling together." He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter Toyosukiirihime, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and delegated the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] to another daughter, {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}},{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became [[:en:Emaciated|Emaciated]] after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |issn=0304-1042}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]].{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed [[:en:Futomani|divination]], which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased. After this [[Ōyamato Shrine]] was founded for Yamato Okunitama{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|大和神社|version=100985927|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] }} [[en:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[ja:大和神社]] [[ja:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and [[sn:commons:Category:Ohyamato-jinja]][[en:Ōyamato Shrine]][[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]][[ja:大和神社]][[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]][[zh:大和神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q245731]] Wikidata name: Ōyamato Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōyamato Shrine
大和神社 | image = File:Oyamato-jinja, haiden.jpg | caption = The ''Haiden'', or main prayer hall. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57083333|N|135.8375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|WD=Q11387080}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 306 Hoshiyama, Shinsencho, [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] | website = {{URL|http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Shrine'''|大和神社|''Ōyamato Jinja''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Monument of Japanese battleship Yamato in Oyamato Shrine.jpg|thumb|262px|Monument of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]] in Ōyamato Shrine]] In the time of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] there was a crisis. Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]], the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace. When a series of plagues broke out during [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin's]] reign, he "dreaded [...] the power of these Gods, and did not feel secure in their dwelling together." He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter Toyosukiirihime, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and delegated the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] to another daughter, {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}},{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became [[:en:Emaciated|Emaciated]] after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |issn=0304-1042}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]].{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed [[:en:Futomani|divination]], which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased. After this [[Ōyamato Shrine]] was founded for Yamato Okunitama{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato 大和神社 | image = File:Oyamato-jinja, haiden.jpg | caption = The ''Haiden'', or main prayer hall. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57083333|N|135.8375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|WD=Q11387080}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 306 Hoshiyama, Shinsencho, [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] | website = {{URL|http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Shrine'''|大和神社|''Ōyamato Jinja''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Monument of Japanese battleship Yamato in Oyamato Shrine.jpg|thumb|262px|Monument of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]] in Ōyamato Shrine]] In the time of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] there was a crisis. Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]], the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace. When a series of plagues broke out during [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin's]] reign, he "dreaded [...] the power of these Gods, and did not feel secure in their dwelling together." He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter Toyosukiirihime, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and delegated the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] to another daughter, {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}},{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became [[:en:Emaciated|Emaciated]] after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |issn=0304-1042}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]].{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed [[:en:Futomani|divination]], which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased. After this [[Ōyamato Shrine]] was founded for Yamato Okunitama{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] }} [[en:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[ja:大和神社]] [[ja:Ōyamato B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] }} [[en:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[ja:大和神社]] [[ja:Ōyamato [[sn:commons:Category:Ohyamato-jinja]][[en:Ōyamato Shrine]][[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]][[ja:大和神社]][[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]][[zh:大和神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q245731]] Wikidata name: Ōyamato Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōyamato Shrine
大和神社 | image = File:Oyamato-jinja, haiden.jpg | caption = The ''Haiden'', or main prayer hall. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57083333|N|135.8375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|WD=Q11387080}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 306 Hoshiyama, Shinsencho, [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] | website = {{URL|http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Shrine'''|大和神社|''Ōyamato Jinja''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Monument of Japanese battleship Yamato in Oyamato Shrine.jpg|thumb|262px|Monument of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]] in Ōyamato Shrine]] In the time of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] there was a crisis. Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]], the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace. When a series of plagues broke out during [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin's]] reign, he "dreaded [...] the power of these Gods, and did not feel secure in their dwelling together." He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter Toyosukiirihime, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and delegated the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] to another daughter, {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}},{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became [[:en:Emaciated|Emaciated]] after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |issn=0304-1042}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]].{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed [[:en:Futomani|divination]], which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased. After this [[Ōyamato Shrine]] was founded for Yamato Okunitama{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and [[sn:commons:Category:Ohyamato-jinja]][[en:Ōyamato Shrine]][[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]][[ja:大和神社]][[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]][[zh:大和神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q245731]] Wikidata name: Ōyamato Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:42, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Ōyamato Shrine
大和神社 | image = File:Oyamato-jinja, haiden.jpg | caption = The ''Haiden'', or main prayer hall. | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.57083333|N|135.8375|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Toshigami|WD=Q3028663}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|WD=Q11387080}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 306 Hoshiyama, Shinsencho, [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] | website = {{URL|http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = | leadership = | prefecture= {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ōyamato Shrine'''|大和神社|''Ōyamato Jinja''}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. [[File:Monument of Japanese battleship Yamato in Oyamato Shrine.jpg|thumb|262px|Monument of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]] in Ōyamato Shrine]] In the time of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] there was a crisis. Amaterasu ([[Shintai|via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword]]) and [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]], the [[:en:Tutelary deity|Tutelary deity]] of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace. When a series of plagues broke out during [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin's]] reign, he "dreaded [...] the power of these Gods, and did not feel secure in their dwelling together." He thus entrusted the mirror and the sword to his daughter Toyosukiirihime, who brought them to the village of Kasanuhi, and delegated the worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] to another daughter, {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}},{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston}}{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became [[:en:Emaciated|Emaciated]] after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.{{Cite journal |last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |issn=0304-1042}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]].{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}} This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion. When the pestilence showed no sign of abating, he then performed [[:en:Futomani|divination]], which revealed the plague to have been caused by [[Ōmononushi]], the god of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]]. When the god was offered proper worship as per his demands, the epidemic ceased. After this [[Ōyamato Shrine]] was founded for Yamato Okunitama{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|大和神社|version=100985927|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] }} [[en:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[ja:大和神社]] [[ja:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Yamato Province]] [[Category:Buildings in Tenri, Nara]] [[Category:History of Tenri, Nara]] [[Category:Yamanobe-no-michi]] {{Commons category|Ohyamato-jinja}} [[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]] [[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[zh:大和神社]] {{Commons category|Ohyamato-jinja}} [[fr:Ōyamato-jinja]] [[ru:Ооямато-дзиндзя]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[zh:大和神社]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q245731}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q245731}}

B. |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Studies-Shinto-Shrines-R-Ponsonby-Fane/dp/1138983225 |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=en |chapter=Oyamato Jinja}} and the descendants of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] serve the shrine to this day. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].{{cite book |last1=Grapard |first1=Allan G. |editor1-last=Breen |editor1-first=John|editor-link1=John Breen (scholar) |editor2-last=Teeuwen |editor2-first=Mark|editor-link2=Mark Teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |date=2000 |publisher=[[University of Hawaii Press]] |isbn=9780824823634 |pages=74–75 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC |chapter=Chaper 5. The economics of ritual power. The twenty-two shrines system}} In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian {{transl|ja|kami}} of Japan. These {{transl|ja|heihaku}} were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōyamato Shrine.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=Studies in Shintō and shrines : papers selected from the works of R.A.B. Ponsonby-Fane |date=1962 |publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |pages=116–117|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ|oclc=3994492}} From 1871 through 1946, the Ōyamato Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.{{cite book |last1=Ponsonby-Fane |first1=Richard |authorlink1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane |title=The Imperial House of Japan |date=1959 |publisher=The Ponsonby Memorial Society |location=Kyoto |page=124|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ|oclc=194887}} The shrine was a guardian shrine of [[:en:Japanese battleship Yamato|Japanese battleship Yamato]].{{cite web |title=Official site |url=http://www5.plala.or.jp/ooyamato/ |website=www5.plala.or.jp |language=ja}} == See also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category|Ohyamato-jinja}} {{reflist|2}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Miwa and Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] }} [[en:Ōyamato Shrine]] [[ja:大和神社]] [[ja:Ōyamato
